coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and electrochemical kinetics studies of reduced carbon quantum dots- supported palladium nanoparticles as bifunctional catalysts in methanol oxidation and hydrogen evolution reactions
coredata.dc:creator: Javan, Hakimeh
Asghari, Elnaz
Ashassi-Sorkhabi, Habib
coredata.dc:description: Glassy carbon electrode, GCE, is decorated with an electrochemically-deposited layer of reduced carbon quantum dots, RCQDs. The Pd nanoparticles are then electrodeposited on RCQDs and the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE is fabricated. The fabricated electrodes are characterized by some characterization techniques. The electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation reaction, MOR, and hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, are investigated using different electrochemical measurement techniques in alkaline and acidic media, respectivly. Cyclic voltammetry, CV, Linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, Chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS, measurements are used to evaluate the bifunctional catalytic behavior of the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. All results are compared with Pd-NPs/GCE and the effect of decorating the GCE substrate with RCQDs is discussed. The results indicate that modification of GCE with a layer of RCQDs increases the active surface area from 0.12 for Pd-NPs/GCE to 0.22 cm2 for Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. Also Eonset and EIS parameters are calculated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical synthesis
Nanocomposite
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Methanol oxidation
Polarization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs/GCE and Pd- NPs/ CQDs/GCE modified electrode
Electrochemical measurements for investigation of the catalytic performance of the prepared electrodes
Electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation
Electrocatalytic behavior of the electrodes for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of CQDs solution.
TEM images a) CQDs and b) Pd NPs supported on RCQDs.
FE-SEM image of (a) electrodeposited Pd-NPs on GCE and (b) electrodeposited Pd-NPs on palladium nanoparticles on reduced carbon quantum dots.
UV–vis and Photoluminescence emission spectra of CQDs in the aqueous medium.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectra of graphite powder, synthesized CQDs and Pd-NPs/RCQD.
Cyclic voltammograms recorded during electrodeposition of CQDs on glassy carbon electrode at a scan rate of 25 mV.s−1.
Cyclic voltammograms of prepared electrodes in (a) 0.5 M KOH solution (b) 5 mM Fe(CN)6 and 0.5 M KCl solution at scan rate of 50 mV/s.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of methanol oxidation on prepared electrodes in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH at scan rate of 50 mV.s−1 (b) Tafel plots of electrodes for oxidation of methanol at room temperature.
The effect of electrolyte temperature on the catalytic activity of a) Pd-NPs/GCE and b) Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH at scan rate of 5 mV.s−1.
Arrhenius plots based on i˳, for Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE at different temperatures for methanol oxidation reaction.
Cyclic voltammograms of (a) Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE and (b) Pd-NPs/GCE at different concentrations of methanol in 0.50 M KOH at scan rate of 50 mV.s−1.
Chronoamperograms of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE and Pd-NPs/GCE at -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH.
Nyquist plots of Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M methanol solution at -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
(a) Linear sweep voltammograms and (b) cathodic region of Tafel plots for HER on RCQD/GCE, Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE at scan rate of 5 mV/s in the 0.5 M solution of H2SO4.
Polarization curves of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE before and after 800 cycles in 0.50 M H2SO4 at a scan rate of 5 mV/s.
The effect of temperature on the behavior of (a) Pd-NPs/GCE and (b) Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 at scan rate of 5 mV.s−1.
Arrhenius plots for Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE at different temperature for hydrogen evolution reaction.
Nyquist diagram of a) Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE at three different voltages of -0.25 V, -0.3 V and -0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl and b) Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE at E= -0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
Kinetic parameters for methanol oxidation on two different electrodes in 0.5 M KOH and + 1 M CH3OH.
Comparison of some of the parameters for the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE with other works.
Electrochemical impedance parameters of Pd/GCE and Pd–NPs/RCQD/GCE electrocatalysts in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M methanol solution at -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
Kinetic parameters for hydrogen evolution on two prepared electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

Pt

head.ce:title: Fabrication and electrochemical kinetics studies of reduced carbon quantum dots- supported palladium nanoparticles as bifunctional catalysts in methanol oxidation and hydrogen evolution reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glassy carbon electrode, GCE, is decorated with an electrochemically-deposited layer of reduced carbon quantum dots, RCQDs. The Pd nanoparticles are then electrodeposited on RCQDs and the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE is fabricated. The fabricated electrodes are characterized by some characterization techniques. The electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation reaction, MOR, and hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, are investigated using different electrochemical measurement techniques in alkaline and acidic media, respectivly. Cyclic voltammetry, CV, Linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, Chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS, measurements are used to evaluate the bifunctional catalytic behavior of the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. All results are compared with Pd-NPs/GCE and the effect of decorating the GCE substrate with RCQDs is discussed. The results indicate that modification of GCE with a layer of RCQDs increases the active surface area from 0.12 for Pd-NPs/GCE to 0.22 cm2 for Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. Also Eonset and EIS parameters are calculated.

ce:section.ce:para: During recent decades, efforts for replacing the fossil fuels with renewable and green energy resources have been the big contributions of scientific researches. The common green energy systems are consisted of fuel cells, solar cells, batteries, supercapacitors and etc. Different energy production reactions such as oxidation of small alcohols [1], reduction of oxygen [2], evolution and oxidation of hydrogen (HER and HOR) [3,4] can be performed depending on the type of system.

The alcohol oxidation because of its acceptable power density, facile system, low operating temperature, safety handling, application of low-cost liquid fuels, has attracted much attention [5,6]. Methanol is the lightest alcohol and is manufactured through various petrochemical routes. It has widely been used as the fuel of the commonly found direct methanol fuel cell, DMFC,s [1]. Its oxidation is kinetically slow and must be catalyzed to yield an appropriate conversion efficiency [7].

Hydrogen is also being forcefully applied as a future energy carrier due to its green and environmentally friendly features. The high requirements for renewable energy can be the main reason for such an increasing attention to these reactions [4]. Hydrogen gas can be produced from electrochemical reduction of water molecules as well as other chemical routes. The hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, from electroreduction of water (or H+ ions) is a green technology because of less chemicals consumption of the process; however, it is also kinetically slow and requires to be catalyzed [8].

As mentioned above, similar to other redox reactions, the major challenge of the scientists with these systems is their low efficiencies. Because of the slow kinetics of these reactions, the limited efficiencies can be overcome by the use of catalysts. Different catalysts have been introduced and applied in these systems. Platinum is the best one, but it also has some disadvantages like high cost and high tendency to the poisoning. [9–15] So, these limitations have forced researchers to find non- Pt catalysts [16,17] or alloys of Pt with some electroactive metals such as Pd [18–20], Ni [21,22], Cu [23,24], Au [25,26], and etc. Among these different candidates, palladium-based electrocatalysts have attracted the attention of scientists for applications in different systems. In the mid-1990s, the favorable price of palladium compared with platinum was an initial reason for the development of palladium-based catalyst systems. In fact the lower price of the palladium at mid-1990 was because of lower demand for it. It promoted the scientists to use palladium instead of platinum. The increased application of the Pd caused a significant enhancement of its price during recent years. However, Pd showed less poisoning during catalytic processes and it was the main reason of using it in electrocatalysis purposes nowadays [27].

The carbon- based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots (CQD) have also been used along with metal-based nanomaterials for improving their electrical properties and catalytic characteristics. Among these, CQDs are quite new nanomaterials and have rarely investigated in electrocatalytic systems. [28–33] Because of unique physical, chemical, optical and electrical properties, CQDs have been attracted much attention during recent years. The most interesting point about CQDs is their accidental discovery. At 2004, Xu and coworkers [34], find out a mixture of fluorescent nanoparticles accidentally during the separation and purification of single walled carbon nanotube. These nanomaterials can be synthesized through simple synthesis routes like laser ablation [35,36], chemical and electrochemical oxidation [37–40], thermal decomposition [41], microwave irradiation [42,43], hydrothermal treatment [44,45], plasma treatment [46] and ultrasonic synthesis [47,48]. Among these methods, electrochemical oxidation has several advantages such as high purity of products, high efficiency, good reproduction capability, neglected carbonaceous aggregations, acceptable stability and controllable size of the products [49–51]. CQDs have various applications in bio-medical delivery systems, bio-sensing, bio-imaging, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, light emitting devices, chemical sensors and etc. Among these applications, using the CQDs in conjunction with metallic nanoparticles in order to catalyze the electrochemical reactions has attracted the attention of many scientists in the recent decades. But there is only limited researches in which CQDs have been used with metallic nanoparticles as an electrocatalyst. [30,31,33]

In the present work, CQDs were synthesized through a simple electrochemical method from a graphite rod. The synthesized CQDs along with Pd nanoparticles (Pd-NPs) were then electrodeposited directly on glassy carbon electrode, GCE. The electrocatalytic activities of the prepared electrodes for catalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, in acidic media and methanol oxidation reaction, MOR, in alkaline media were evaluated using electrochemical techniques. The cyclic voltammetry, CV, linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, Chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS, were the electrochemical techniques used for evaluation of the catalytic properties of the electrodes.

Palladium chloride (PdCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), sulfuric acid 98% (H2SO4) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were purchased from Merck Co. A three-electrode system consisted of a glassy carbon electrode, GCE, platinum plate and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) respectively as working, counter and reference electrodes were used.

Preparation of CQDs was performed by a facile and cost-effective electrochemical method in a two-electrode cell using the ADAK DC power supply. In this system, two graphite rods, placed parallel to each other with a constant distance of 7 cm, were used as cathode and anode. Subsequently, CQDs were synthesized by exfoliation of a graphite rod immersed in the 200 mL of ultrapure water at applied voltages between 30–40 V. The reaction occurred under stirring for 5 days to obtain a dark- brown homogenous mixture. In order to have a pure CQDs solution a slow speed filter paper was used. The mixture was then centrifuged at 22,000 rpm for 30 min for separation of precipitated graphite particles. [52,53]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/GCE and Pd- NPs/ CQDs/GCE modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Before every electrochemical deposition, GCE was polished with standard polishing papers and cleaned with distilled water carefully. Then, synthesized CQDs were deposited on GCE directly via an electrochemical route. For this purpose, a mixture of KCl (0.10 M) with CQDs (1.0 mg.mL−1) was prepared and the CQDs were dispersed in the solution using an ultrasonic bath (BANDELIN SONOREX DIGITEC) for 1 h. The pH of the mixture was then adjusted to 4 (pH meter 654 metrohm). CQDs were then deposited on GCE by cyclic voltammetry in the voltage range of -1.2 to 0.50 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at scan rate of 25 mV/s for 20 cycles. The palladium nanoparticles were also deposited on the CQD decorated GCE using the same procedure i.e. cyclic voltammetry in the voltage range of -1.0 to 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at scan rate of 50 mV/s for 10 cycles. For this purpose, the 0.20 mM solution of PdCl2 in 0.10 M KCl were used as electrolyte.

FTIR spectra of CQDs were obtained at the range of 400-4000 cm−1 by a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscope (Tensor 27 from Germany Brulker Co) with a resolution of 4 cm−1 with a minimum of 256 scans per spectrum; the measurements were performed at room temperature. The procedure was done under nitrogen gas atmosphere to prevent humidity interference.

The morphology of water dispersed catalysts was evaluated by an EM10C-100 KV (Zeiss, Germany) transition electron microscope, TEM, and the morphology of prepared electrodes was studied using a Field-Emission scanning electron microscope, FE-SEM (TESCAN, MIRAIII, Czech Republic).

The absorption spectrum of CQDs recorded by a UV–vis Analytic Jena Specord 250 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm and the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded by LS-45 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from PerkinElmer Co. Crystallographic patterns of graphite, CQDs and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE were assessed by X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS model D500) with a Cu tube anode. The X-ray diffractogram was scanned with the diffraction angle increasing from 2θ = 2° to 2θ = 70°. Raman spectroscopy was also carried out using a Teksan Takram P50C0R10 spectrometer with a laser wavelength of 532 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements for investigation of the catalytic performance of the prepared electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: All of the electrochemical experiments were performed using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT30 potentiostat– galvanostat. The cyclic voltammetry for calculating active surface area was done in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 0.5 M KCl solution at potential range from -0.5 to 0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl with scan rate of 50 mV.s−1.

All of the MOR tests were done in alkaline media consisted of 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH at room temperature. Cyclic voltammograms in methanol solution were recorded at potential rang of -1.0 to 0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl with scan rate of 50 mV.s−1. Linear sweep voltammograms were also recorded in alkaline methanol solution with a scan rate of 5 mV.s−1. Chronoamperometry tests were done at constant potential of -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 7000 s. Finally, EIS tests were carried out at constant voltage of -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl with Autolab potentiostat equipped with a FRA module. The Nyquist plots were fitted to appropriate electrochemical equivalent circuits using the ZView® (II) software and the corresponding parameters were collected. The perturbation voltage should be checked to be low enough to ensure over linear response; so, it was selected to be 10 mV (rms) sinusoidal voltage within the frequency range of 10 kHz to 100 mHz.

The HER tests were done in acidic media consisted of 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. LSV tests were performed in this solution with a scan rate of 5 mV.s−1. EIS measurements were also performed at three different voltages of -0.25 V, -0.3 V and -0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl at frequency range of 10 kHz to 100 mHz. The voltages for EIS tests were selected from LSV results.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the prepared CQDs (Fig. 1 ) verified the presence of different types of functional groups. The broad absorption peak of OH at 3407 cm−1, the stretching vibration band of CO in −COOH at 1651 cm−1, and the stretching vibration bands of C–O at 1076 and 824 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups at the surface of CQDs. Moreover, two obvious absorption peaks at 2977 and 1457 cm−1 are associated with the stretch of vibration C–H and CC, corresponding to the presence of alkyl and aryl groups. [53]

Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis absorption and photo-luminescent (PL) emission spectra of the CQD aqueous mixtures. It can be observed from the UV–vis spectrum that the CQDs show two absorption peaks at around 240 and 360 nm; the first one that is a high and clear peak can be related to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 bond; the second one, that has been merged in the first high peak and can be seen just as a shoulder, is related to the n-π * transitions of the carbonyl functional groups [57,58]. The photo-luminescent spectrum confirms the optical properties of the synthesized CQDs. Presence of a PL peak at around 450 nm verifies the optical properties of CQDs in the range of visible light wavelengths where the excitation wavelength fixed at 340 nm.

Fig. 5 a shows X-ray diffraction, XRD, pattern of graphite, CQDs and Pd-NPs/RCQD. As it can be seen from CQDs pattern, a broad peak located at around 2θ = 17° is related to (002) plane of amorphous carbon nano dots. In fact, by oxidation of graphite and creation of oxygen functional groups the intensity of the C peak gets lower and its full width at half maximum, FWHM, increases due to the small size of prepared CQDs. However, for the Pd-NPs/ CQD, some sharp diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ = 39.95°, 48.52° and 68.21°, which can be indexed to the (111), (200) and (220) facets of the Pd with face-centered-cubic (fcc) lattice crystal structure. Note that the strongest diffraction peak is from Pd (111), which has been found as a very active facet for oxidation of small organic molecules. The Debye–Scherrer equation (eq. 1) was used for calculating the average size of the Pd-NPs/ RCQDs and CQDs.
(1)
D = kλ/ β cos θ
where D is the diameter of the nanoparticles, k is the shape factor (˜0.90), λ(Cu Kα) =0.154059 nm, β is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity (FWHM) and θ is the Bragg’s angle. Based on the (111) diffraction peak in Fig. 5a, the average diameter of the Pd nanoparticles was calculated to be 56.8 nm. While at 2θ = 17, ̊ the average diameter of CQDs was obtained 5.39 nm. [54]

Raman spectroscopy is one of the suitable and wieldy used techniques for evaluating the defects and disorders of carbonic materials. [59–61] Raman spectra of the nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 5b. As it is obvious there are two bands at around 1361 and 1602 cm−1 for both graphite and CQDs, that are attributed to D band (as a breathing mode of k-point phonons of A1g symmetry) and G band (E2g phonon of sp2 bonds of carbon atoms) respectively. [62] The ratio of the intensity of these two peaks, ID/IG, is a criterion of disorder in graphite and CQDs. For CQDs the ratio is about 0.86, which is higher than that of graphite, which means that during oxidation of graphite and synthesis of CQDs, some structural defects has been created. [63] In the spectrum of CQD there is a positive shift in wave number of G band that can be related to the presence of oxygenated functional groups in its structure. By making defect in the structure of CQDs, these two bands get wider and their intensity raises [64,65]. Pd-NPs/RCQD showed trends rather similar to CQDs, but the ID/IG increased to 0.91, which means that structural defects increase.

The typical cyclic voltammograms recorded during electrodeposition of CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 6 . As it is obvious there are one anodic peak (I) and two cathodic peaks (II and III) in CV diagrams. By sequential scans, the peak current also increases due to the deposition of CQDs on the electrode surface. Peaks I and II are ascribed to the redox reactions of some oxygenated functional groups of CQDs; these groups are too stable to be reduced completely under these conditions. However, peak III at around -1.0 V doesn’t have an anodic corresponding peak, which means that reduction of CQDs to RCQDs is irreversible. So, this transformation can be a convincing justification for deposition of CQDs on the surface of GCE [66,67].

The electrochemical activity of prepared electrodes, Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE, was examined by cyclic voltammetry in 0.50 M KOH solution. The CV responses that are given in Fig. 7 a, show the general characteristic features of palladium. The cathodic peak at −0.80 V is related to the adsorption of hydrogen on the surface of Pd- NPs and the anodic peak at around -0.50 V is appeared due to hydrogen desorption from electrode surface. The smaller anodic peaks at the voltages of -0.30 V to -0.10 V are attributed to the formation of Pd surface oxides, whereas the reduction of these oxides results in a well-defined cathodic peak between -0.20 V to -0.40 V. All observed peaks for the Pd-NPs/RCQDs/GCE electrode are sharper than those of the Pd-NPs/GCE, indicating that CQDs due to their high porosity, are able to provide the highly active sites for deposition of nanoparticles. [68,69] Also, the life time stability of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE electrode was studied by successive CV scans up to 1000 cycles. As it is obvious from Fig. 7a the CVs don’t show any significant changes after 1000 cycles of scans.

The electrocatalytic activity of electrodes was also studied by cyclic voltammetry in a 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 and 0.50 M KCl solution (Fig. 7b). The electroactive area of the electrodes are calculated by Randles- Sevick equation.
(2)
i p
= 2.99
x
10 5
n A C
D ν
2
where i p, n, A, C, D, and ν are the oxidation peak current density, number of electrons transferred in the reaction, electroactive surface area, concentration of the reactant, diffusion coefficient of the reactant species and the scan rate, respectively. The redox reaction of K4[Fe(CN)6] involves one-electron transfer (n = 1), and the diffusion coefficient is 6.30 × 10 −6 cm 2 s −1. [70,71]

It can be seen from Fig. 7b that the peak current density for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE is higher than others. We can see that the electroactive surface area of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE (0.22 cm2) is larger than Pd-NPs/GCE (0.12 cm2) and bare GCE (0.071 cm2). In fact in the presence of CQDs with incorporated Pd-NPs, electrocatalytic activity increased because of enhancing the available electroactive surface area and promoting electron transfer process compared with bare GCE [72,73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The activity of prepared electrodes toward electrooxidation of methanol in 0.5 M KOH was investigated by CV and compared with each other. As it can be seen from CV curves (Fig. 8 a) there are two oxidation peaks in forward and backward scans. At forward scan, because of some generated intermediate species like CO, oxidation of methanol doesn’t take place properly and completely. In fact, these intermediate species occupy the electroactive sites on palladium surface, leading to the poisoning of the surface; thereupon, disrupt the oxidation process. During the backward scan the carbonaceous species are oxidized to the species like CO2 that are desorbed easily from the surface of the catalyst.

There are some possible pathways for MOR; however, the generally accepted mechanism in alkaline media is presented here [74,75]:
(3-a)
1 )
P d +
O H
-
→ P d -
O H
a d s
+ e
(3-b)
2 )
P d + C
H 3
O H
→ P d −
C
H 3
O H
a d s
(3-c)
3 )
P d −
C
H 3
O H
a d s
+
3 O
H −
→ P d −
C O H
a d s
+ 3
H 2
O + 3
e −
(3-d)
4 )
P d −
C O H
a d s
+
P d − O
H
a d s
→ 2 P d + C H O O H

r.d.s 1
1 Rate determining step
(3-e)
5 )
C H O O H +
O H
-
→ C H O
O -
+
H 2
O

Overall reaction:
(3-f)
C
H 3
O H + 5
O H
-
→
C H O
O -
+ 4
H 2
O + 4
e -

According to the reports, [75–77] the kinetic of methanol oxidation in alkaline media is better than acidic media, which can be due to the lower corrosivity of alkaline solution and reduced alcohol cross-over, subsequently reduced poisoning of the catalyst.

As it is obvious from Fig. 8a, the onset potential of methanol oxidation for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE and Pd-NPs/GCE are -0.50 V and -0.40 V, respectively. The peak current density of the MOR on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE is also ca. four times higher than that of the Pd-NP/GCE. Furthermore, the catalytic current on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE begins to increase sharply at more negative voltages which can lead to a higher efficiency.

Tafel plots of the MOR on the Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE derived from the linear sweep voltammograms in the solution of 0.50 M KOH and 0.50 M methanol at a scanning rate of 5 mV.s−1 are shown in Fig. 8b. Anodic Tafel slope as an important and decisive parameter, was obtained following Tafel Eq. (4):
(4)
ƞ = b l o g
J
+ a
where ƞ , b and J are the overvoltage, Tafel slope and current density, respectively. Table 1 lists some of the parameters which have been extracted from CV and Tafel plots. These obtained kinetic parameters were compared with some other similar researches in Table 2 . As it is obvious from data in Table 1, the peak potential is more negative for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE and the peak current density is about 5 times higher for this electrode with respect to Pd-NP/GCE. The anodic Tafel slopes are high because of the polarization on both electrodes; however, it is lower for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE that is due to the lower diffusion polarization because of higher active surface area of this electrode [68,78].

As it can be seen from Fig. 9, temperature has a positive effect on the electrocatalytic behavior of catalysts. As temperature rises onset potential decreases and the current density of methanol oxidation increases. However, this enhancement is more considerable for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE than its changes for Pd-NPs/GCE. The activation energy, Ea , from the Arrhenius equation is obtained using Eqs. (5) and (6) [79,80]:
(5)
k ( T ) = A
e
- E a / R T
(6)
E a =
- 2.303 R [ d l o g i ˳ / d ( 1 / T ) ]
Where k (T), A and T are the rate constant, pre-exponential factor and temperature, respectively. In electrochemical systems the k (T) is replaced by exchange current density, i˳; and the activation energy normally is obtained based on the i˳, calculated from Tafel plots. The activation energies for both electrodes were obtained from the modified Arrhenius plots (logi˳ vs. (1/T) shown in Fig. 10 ), and summarized in Table 1. Results show that the activation energy for oxidation of methanol on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE (44.03 kJ.mol−1) is lower than that of Pd-NPs/GCE (68.54 kJ.mol-1) that is attributed to the catalytic performance of RCQDs. In fact, the incorporation of RCQDs in the electrode structure not only increases the active surface area of the electrode but also they have a catalytic effect too. So that, the Ea for MOR decreases in some extent in the presence of these particles.

Since generation of some poisoning particles disrupts the process, investigation of the long term stability of prepared catalysts is so important. In this work, the long term stability of the electrodes was examined by Chronoamperometry, CA, technique at constant potential of -0.3 V. the CA curves are shown in Fig. 12 . As it is obvious, the obtained current densities for oxidation of methanol on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE are considerably higher than those of Pd-NPs/GCE; it is attributed to the enhanced porosity, surface area and catalytic activity provided by CQDs. Also, CQDs having a lot of oxygenated groups on their surface, prevent the surface poisoning by oxidizing the CO to CO2. It can be concluded that the CQDs as a supporting material can improve the electrocatalytic activity and prevent CO poisoning tolerance of the Pd-NPs significantly.

In order to evaluate the physical and chemical interface procedures at the electrode surface, we conducted the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE electrocatalysts. The as-prepared catalysts were examined by EIS at different voltages in 0.5 M KOH aqueous solution containing 0.5 M CH3OH (Fig. 13 ). Two greatly overlapped and depressed semicircles appeared in the Nyquist plots of Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd–NPs/RCQD/GCE in the high and low frequency regions. At high frequency region, the appeared partly small semicircle fitted with charge transfer resistance, Rct, in parallel with constant phase element, CPE that can be related to double layer capacitance. During the oxidation of methanol, some intermediate species like COads cover the active sites of Pd and prevent further adsorption of methanol molecules on the surface. This process may result in a low-frequency feature in Nyquist plots that is simulated using a parallel set of a resistor, R ads, and a capacitor, C ads, in series with R ct. [86,87] The experimental data were fitted and the values of its electric elements were recorded in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic behavior of the electrodes for hydrogen evolution reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
At the next step the electrocatalytic activity of samples toward hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, in acidic media was studied. Linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, tests were carried out in the 0.50 M H2SO4 using the fabricated nanocomposite electrodes. In acidic media there are three well- determined steps for HER [88]:
(7-a)
1 )
H +
+ e
→
H
a d s
V o l m e r
r e a c t i o n
(7-b)
2 )
H
a d s
+
H
+
+ e
→
H 2
H e y r o v s k y
r e a c t i o n
(7-c)
3 )
H
a d s
+
H
a d s
→
H 2
T a f e l
r e a c t i o n

According to the LSV voltammograms illustrated in Fig. 14 a, it is obvious that, remarkable improvement in onset potential achieved in the presence of RCQDs. It indicated a shift from ˜ -0.06 V for Pd-NPs/GCE to ˜ +0.03 V for Pd-NPs/ RCQD/GCE; it means that required energy for initiation of HER on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE is less than those on Pd-NPs/GCE and RCQDs/GCE. This positive shift which is attributed to high porosity and active surface area of the electrode induced by simultaneous presence of RCQDs and Pd-NPs and is comparable with commercial standard electrodes. The improved catalytic activity of the electrode was also confirmed by comparison of current densities in a specific potential. For investigation of reaction kinetics, cathodic region of Tafel plots were derived from LSV voltammograms (presented in Fig. 14b). The cathodic Tafel slopes were extracted and are listed in Table 4 . As it is clear from Table 4, the cathodic Tafel slope is lowest for HER on Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE. It was obtained ca. 45 mV/dec that is corresponding to the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism. [33,89]. Also this calculated parameters were compared with some other research work in Table 5 .

The stability of the electrodes was also investigated by successive performing of the LSV voltammetry up to 800 cycles. As it is observed from Fig. 15 , there is no significant difference between the LSV plot before cycling and the plot recorded after 800 cycles that verifies stability of the electrode under operating conditions.

The effect of temperature on the behavior of the electrodes was also studied. The LSV voltammograms recorded at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 16 . As it is expected, temperature has an incremental effect on reaction kinetics. So that, at high temperatures the onset potential shifts toward positive values. For more understanding of reaction kinetics, the activation energies of HER on the fabricated electrodes were calculated after extraction of i˳ values from corresponding Tafel plots. The Arrhenius plots were then drawn and the Ea values were calculated according to these plots (Fig. 17 ) and Eqs (5) and (6). It was deduced that for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE electrocatalyst Ea is 58.6 kJ.mol−1 while it is 81.2 kJ.mol-1 for Pd-NPs/GCE. The lower value of activation energy is considered to be due to the providing higher active surface area by RCQDs as well as the catalytic performance of these particles as a co-catalyst beside Pd-NPs [90–92].

For investigation of the HER kinetics on electrodes, the Nyquist diagrams at three different potentials for Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE carried out in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution and are presented in Fig. 18 . Also, the suggested equivalent circuit model for simulating the experimental data is shown as an inset on the same figuret. In the circuit model, R 1 is related to the ohmic resistance of system that is approximately constant and independent from applied voltages. The CPE1 and R 2 represent the constant phase element of electrical double layer capacitance and charge transfer resistance, respectively; they have been used to simulate the electrochemical process at the interface between particles and electrolyte during the reaction. [97] The fitting results showed that the values of R 2 gradually decreased by accompanying with the increases of voltage, so that its value decreased from 86 Ω at E= -0.25 V, to 30 Ω at E= -0.35 V.

During performing EIS tests at low frequencies released hydrogen bubbles stuck on the surface of the electrode were observed, especially at higher applied voltages. These persistent strongly-stuck bubbles caused a low-frequency feature at Nyquist plots, which was modeled using a resistance, R 3 and CPE2, in parallel.

The Nyquist diagrams of two prepared electrodes, Pd-NPs/GCE and Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE, are shown in Fig. 18b. The lower resistance of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE nanocomposite toward charge transfer is evident from this figure. It is mainly due to the higher active surface area of the electrode because of incorporated RCQDs layer on GCE.

In summary, we prepared Pd-NPs based on RCQDs nanocomposite by a facile one-step electrochemical method and investigated its behavior as a catalyst for HER and MOR systems. Electrochemical investigations showed that presence of RCQDs, caused improvement of electrocatalytic activity of the electrode. This improvement indicated its effects by changing the onset potentials of HER as well as MOR. Also, the calculated kinetic parameters such as Tafel slopes confirmed the improving effects of the synthesized carbonic nanomaterial on the electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes. Effect of temperature on kinetics of reactions showed that activation energies in the presence of RCQDs as a support material, were considerably lower than other electrodes. According to the results, the enhanced catalytic activity of Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE catalyst can be attributed to the presence of carbon quantum dots which increases the electronic conductivity of the electrodes, provides high active surface area and introduces an additional catalytic performance beside the catalytic action of Pd-NPs as well. The results of stability investigations, confirmed the superiority of proposed Pd-NPs/RCQD/GCE nanocomposite because of its acceptable stability in successive cycling scans of the potential. Finally the EIS investigations showed an improved electrocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite in both MOR and HER systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was carried out in “Electrochemistry Research Laboratory” of “University of Tabriz’’. The authors would like to thank “University of Tabriz” for its financial supports of this project.
